does: This paper presents an update to circuits research methodology, focusing on understanding neural network features by analyzing their downstream connections. The work extends the practice of mechanistic interpretability by tracing how individual features propagate through network layers and influence downstream computations. Rather than examining features in isolation, the authors systematically map feature interactions and their causal effects on model behavior. The update includes refined techniques for identifying which downstream neurons or attention heads receive significant input from specific features, along with methods for quantifying the importance of these connections. The paper demonstrates these approaches on transformer models, showing how features detected at intermediate layers flow into and modify later computations that directly affect model outputs.
connects: This work builds directly on the mechanistic interpretability and circuits research programs established over recent years, which sought to reverse-engineer neural networks by identifying interpretable computational subgraphs. It extends earlier feature attribution and neural circuit mapping approaches that identified individual neurons or attention heads performing interpretable tasks. The downstream-connection focus responds to limitations in purely layer-by-layer analysis, moving beyond treating features atomistically toward understanding their functional roles in broader computational graphs. This continues the line of work examining how information flows through networks and which connections matter for behavior, previously explored through path ranking and indirect effect analysis. The paper builds on accumulated insights about attention patterns, feature mixing, and redundancy in transformer computations.
enables: This methodological advancement enables more complete understanding of how transformer models implement their computations, potentially revealing editing targets for model steering and safety applications. By mapping downstream feature flow, researchers can identify which features drive specific model behaviors and potentially intervene on them. The approach provides tools for comparing how different model scales, architectures, or training procedures route information differently, supporting principled model comparison. These techniques could facilitate automated circuit discovery and enable more targeted explanations of model misbehavior, supporting efforts to identify problematic learned circuits before deployment. The downstream-connection mapping may also improve sparse autoencoders and other interpretability tools by providing supervision for learning meaningful feature decompositions aligned with actual computational importance in networks.
